lessonTitle: Labour and Delivery and Post Partum in Sickle Cell Disease

items.html: Increase awareness of guidance for optimizing birth
Identify and reduce risks in postpartum care
Monitoring: • Fetal - continuous electronic fetal monitoring • Maternal - vitals including SpO2 (> 95%), maintain fluid balance, analgesia, warmth
Medications: • Early neuraxial anesthesia to minimize pain and anxiety
Consultations: • OB/MFM • Haematology • Anaesthesia • Neonatology

Best Option for Delivery: • Delivery should take place in an expert centre, with availability of relevant maternal and fetal monitoring • Aiming for spontaneous vaginal delivery • Cesarian section, reserved for obstetrical indication

The Little Things: • Maternal hydration should be emphasized, either via the oral or intravenous route • Maintenance of maternal warmth and comfort • Adequate pain relief • Supplemental oxygen, as required • Spinal anaesthesia is recommended to reduce post partum VOE

before.heading: Recommendation
before.text: Based on the American Society of Hematology (ASH) 2020 guidelines, it is recommended that labour be induced between 38 and 40 weeks.

after.heading: Rationale
after.text: Rationale: to decrease perinatal mortality, particularly for patients with SCD, related to placental abnormalities

items.html: Good analgesia to manage pain
Epidural analgesia is safe and effective for pregnant women with SCD.
Avoid dehydration to reduce risk of sickling crisis
Regular monitoring of oxygen saturation
Avoid protracted/prolonged labour.
Continuous electronic fetal monitoring due to increased risk of unexplained stillbirth and adverse perinatal outcomes for pregnant women with SCD.

before.heading: Preferred
before.text: Regional anesthesia is preferred for Cesarean section

after.heading: Higher Risk
after.text: General anesthesia is associated with higher risks of sickling complications

data.bubble_text: Vigilance around pain management is essential in the immediate post partum period. An acute pain crisis after delivery happens in approximately 25% of women. Hydration, oxygenation and early mobilization reduce pain crisis risk.

items.title: Breastfeeding
items.html: Encourage, except in mothers who require medications that are transferred through breast milk.

items.title: VTE Prophylaxis
items.html: Venous thromboembolism is increased in pregnant patients with sickle cell disease. Offer VTE prophylaxis with Low Molecular Weight Heparin (LMWH) for 3 - 6 weeks.

items.title: Infection Risk
items.html: Monitor for infections such as urinary tract infection, wound infection, or endometritis.

items.title: Post Partum Visit
items.html: • To discuss contraception • Preparation for next pregnancy

items.title: Medications/Contraception
items.html: • Progestin-only methods preferred (estrogen containing contraceptives not completely contraindicated) • Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate may reduce the frequency of acute pain crises • Copper or Progesterone IUD • Progestin implant - thin plastic tube inserted under the skin of upper arm; works for 5 years

items.title: Hydroxyurea use Post Partum
items.html: • 3% (1000 mg or 15-20 mg/kg/day) excreted in breast milk • Less than 5-10% safety threshold • Avoiding breastfeeding for 3 hours can reduce infant dose by about 50% • Discussion of risk vs benefit about initiating hydroxyurea during breastfeeding

data.question: Spinal anaesthesia is recommended to reduce post partum VOE.
data.feedback_correct: Regional (spinal) anaesthesia is preferred over general anaesthesia which is associated with higher risks of sickling complications.

items.html: Optimizing intrapartum care is critical to reducing risks postpartum
The postpartum period offers the opportunity to advise about optimization of future pregnancies and contraception.
Camous J, N'da A, Etienne-Julan M, Stéphan F. Anesthetic management of pregnant women with sickle cell disease--effect on postnatal sickling complications. Can J Anaesth. 2008 May;55(5):276-83
https://www.canhaem.org/wp-content/uploads/2018/09/Contraception-Pre-Conception-Counseling-and-Pregnancy.pdf
https://onlinelibrary-wiley-com.uhn.idm.oclc.org/doi/pdf/10.1111/bjh.13270
Berghella, V. (Ed.). (2017). Maternal-Fetal Evidence Based Guidelines (3rd ed.). CRC Press
